sentences.text: My name is Adam Boren.
Last name is spelled B as in boy, O-R-E, N as Nancy, Boren.
My original name was Abraham Borensztejn spelled B as in boy, O-R-E-N-S-Z, T as in Thomas, E-J-N.
I was born on November 15, 1929.
I am 67 years old.
I was born in Warsaw, Poland.
My father's name was, first name was Israel Borensztejn.
My mother's name was Sara.
Her maiden name Gold.
I had a brother and a sister.
My sister, who was I think either six or seven years older than I, the first child, her name was Mina, M as in Mary, I, N as Nancy, A.
And my brother, his name was Mietek, M as Mary, I-E, T as Thomas, E-K.
He was four years older than I.
I was very close to my sister.
I was in constant battle with my brother, staying out of each other's way whenever possible.
We had totally different characters.
While I was rather wild and very social, he was withdrawn to himself.
And perhaps that our, our moods didn't match we had fights and disagreements.
My sister, when I was a little boy, and I used to read a lot.
I used to read about a book a day when I was a boy, even six, seven.
And I used to fall asleep at a table.
She would pick me up, undress me, put me in bed.
And I woke up in her bed next morning.
And she used to take my side in whatever battle or between my brother and myself, or if my parents would criticize me, or try to punish me, she would always take my side, protect me.
My father was a graduate chemist.
And as far as I have heard in past, he was in several businesses.
In one, he was in a dye processing.
He was in halva manufacturing.
But during my time, he had a manufacturing plant in eastern Poland, Northeastern Poland, of dehydrated fruits, apples and others, and a sawmill.
The town's name was Nowojelnie, spelled N-O-W-E, no, W-O, I'm sorry, J-E-L-N-I-E.
That would be near-- the major town would be Grodno, G-R-O-D-N-O.
Today, it is Belarus.
It was northeastern part of Poland.
Yes, as I mentioned, he also had a sawmill attached to the same factory.
In fact, the wood from the sawmill was used to fire the boilers for the power to drive the machinery for the dehydration and for the sawmill.
Yes, my father was interested that when I grow up and get my education, that I would take over.
In fact, in making dehydrated apples, you skin them, of course, and decore them.
In the skin of an apple is a chemical called pectin, which is a gelling chemical used to making any gelling food.
This was an American invention and process.

My father showed me the powders that if you mix with water becomes Jell-O and wanted me after I graduated from schools to go to the United States and learn the process so that we can utilize the skin, apple skins.
I was young.
It was interesting.
He took good care of me when I went to the factory.
He would leave me there.
I played around.
There were little railroad wagons on which they carried the tree trunks for cutting.
And I would ride those.
I'd get almost killed a few times getting on to the cutting machinery, too curious to look, to see how the wood is being cut.
So I enjoyed it.
First, I was by myself, nobody to tell me anything.
I mean, I had friends there.
I made friends.
I think I was twice or three times in the factory.
About how many people would you estimate worked in the factories for your father?
Oh, I would say probably 100, mostly, the surrounding villages, the peasants.
The women would work at the machines that peel, and core, and slice the apples.
And the men would work in the sawmill.
To best of my knowledge, most of the product, the hydrated apples, and the hydrated pears, and prunes were exported, exported to Germany prior to Hitler, and to England, France.
They were a very high quality.
In fact, the name of the company was California.
The only thought that I had, his father, my grandfather, had a wholesale business of fruits in Warsaw and imported American Washington apples, the red Washington in Poland.
And perhaps that influence from America, I think, the name was California.
No.
No one at work.
OK.
What was some of your father's interests besides the business, would you say?
I really don't know.
My father traveled extensively.
He was, I would say, more than 50%, perhaps even more than 70%, out of the house.
He was traveling throughout Europe.
And I enjoyed that very much, because he used to bring me gifts from all different countries, small gifts.
And he used to tell me stories.
For instance, Romania, which was one of the most corrupted countries in Europe at that time, you could take a train, he told me, from Warsaw to Bucharest, which was the capital of Romania.
And from Bucharest to some small town, an express train that would not stop there.
But if you gave some dollars to the train conductor, he arranged that the train would stop wherever you want it to going, and would stop, picking you up again on the way back.
So those type of stories, he used to tell me, and tell me about the countries, the people.
So it was to me very interesting.
He used bring me, I remember, a pen once that you looked through where then you could see, you turn it and you see different pictures, view little, tiny things.
A wallet once from, I don't know, it was Italy or something, a leather wallet.
Little things, just little things he's doing.
Yes.
My family, my mother and father, were very much interest in Zionists.
In our living room in Warsaw, prominently were displayed picture of Herzl and, and-- oh, my God, my memory-- Weizmann.
And of course, they-- I know my mother, I don't know if my father-- my mother attended meetings, used to take me sometimes to meeting of women Zionist organizations.
No, the only thing I know that he once told me, once or twice, that he has purchased a parcel of land in Palestine.
I don't know where.
I've never researched it, been to Israel many times.
So to that extent, only that I knew that he has some kind of a parcel of land, perhaps thinking that one day, he may immigrate.
No, not to my knowledge.
The pre-war address was Ogrodowa Street, Ogrodowa meaning Polish garden.
It's spelt O-G-R-O-D-O-W-A.
We are ?] Ogrodowa 27.
The apartment was number 14.
It was on the third floor.
I think there were five or six floors all together in the building.
It was a large building, consisting of two central gardens.
The European apartments are somewhat different than the Americans.
When you walk in through the gate, you walk in a garden.
And there was one circular apartment and then another set of apartments are the same, in the same buildings.
I have no idea how many they were, but I know they were either five or six story high, balconies on each apartment.
As I recall it, we had five rooms plus the kitchen.
I slept as a boy with my brother in a room.
We had a room together.
As a little, very little boy, I remember I slept with my mother and my parents, because my father was away most of the time.
I slept with my mother, as a little boy.
And then I slept with my brother when I got older.
Yes, we always had a maid.
A sleep, a sleep-in maid.
Not in particular, but there were young girls, farm girls that came to the city to make money because the economy in Poland was not high.
And there was-- peasants earned very little, if at all any, money.
So that was an opportunity for the girls to earn some money.
They stayed for a year, or half a year, or a year, got the money, and they went back to their villages.
Yes.
I think it was in either '35 or '36.
In fact, the first radio that we had was a crystal radio, being that it's a little tiny thing with a crystal with a long antenna from one building to another.
And you had to play with the crystal to catch the one station, the Warsaw station, which played only a couple, two, three hours a day.
Then came the heterodynes, the tube radios.
And we had those.
And then came super heterodynes, where you had without the whistle.
The first radios, if you turn your dial, it whistled all over between stations.
And then was the better one, so.
Mostly music.
That's what I, there were no, not to my memory, any programs like they were in the United States.
Hi-ho, whatever, there are here, or the soap operas.
Mostly music that I recall.
There was also poetry reading, I recall was on the radio.
Yes.
I remember the numbers too.
I have to translate from Polish.
I remember Polish 23907.
Not in the economic level that we were.
I don't think the working people had phones, but.
I can only compare to apartment I seen visiting France and visiting family.
It was comfortable.
It was comparable.
We had running water, not hot and cold, only cold water.
You had to heat your own water in a tank.
That had bathroom with the water closet, a bath with a tank where you had to heat up the tank of water to have a bath.
There was no hot water running.
Electricity, of course.
We had gas, manufactured gas, not natural.
But we had a very bad incident.
One of the maids that we had a bad love affair and tried to commit suicide, open up all the vents in the kitchen.
My father at night got up and smelled it, because the gas, had the specific smell, and shut it off, of course.
It should have killed us all, but he decided to cut the gas out and we since that time, we had no gas.
We had a stove, coal fire stove and primus burners.
I don't know if you familiar with it.
Primus burner was a Swedish invention.

It was a small burner which had a kerosene tank and you, and a little cup on the top where you put alcohol to start the heating up the burner, the, the end burner and then pumped up the kerosene, and was acting like a gas burner.
In fact, there millions of them were sold.
It was famous in Europe, the primus burner.
Primus is P as Peter, R-I-M-U, S as Sam.
Up to the war, yes.
We, we were, of course, in Europe and Poland specifically, there were only orthodox synagogues and what they called shtiebel, small praying rooms.
Our home was kosher, kept kosher.
Although I was very thin and anemic, supposedly, and one of the doctors prescribed that I should have red meat, and said ham.
So my mother used to get me when I went to school dry roll.
And I used to stop at a Polish delicatessen and buy 10 decagrams of ham.
But never at home, never had anything non-kosher.
We kept all the holidays at home.
My father always, no matter how far he traveled, I always make sure to be home for holidays and also for all the occasions, such as wedding, bar mitzvahs, et cetera.
We also attended, in the same building that we lived in, was a shtiebel, as they called a three- or four-room apartment converted to a praying rooms with a rabbi.
And it was in orthodox tradition.
We had at Sabbath, of course, my mother lit the candles.
When my father was there, excuse me, he would perform the Sabbath service.
But if he wasn't, we just had a dinner.
My mother would light the candles.
We had just a dinner.
I would say two of them.
One was Hanukkah, because we got a lot of gifts from everybody-- from all the relatives the grandparents, the kids got gifts-- and Passover.
First, the whole family was at it.
There was a long dinner to prepare the things.
And we went through Passover for many hours, in fact, till 1 o'clock sometimes in the morning.
And the stories were nice, you know, and of course, we knew Hebrew at the time I was taught.
I forgot most of it.
So we understood everything that we were praying and it was a festive occasion.
And it was different, was different than everydays holiday, different food, different-- of course, matzah rather than bread.
Oh, yes.
I was the youngest, so it was my privilege.
Oh, we had, of course, costumes, and dancing, and all the things normally that.
No.
No, I can't.
I don't think so.
Really nothing.
I can't recall it.
Can't recall.
OK.
How did your family spend summer vacations?

Our vacation from school, when school ended, so we used to usually get out of Warsaw-- not usually, always-- to where the family would rent bungalows, either near Warsaw in, I would say, within 50 miles of Warsaw, there were several resort on the river Vistula.
Wisla in Polish
Wisla is W-I-S, L like Love, A.
And it's called International, it's called Vistula, V-I-S-T-U-L-A river, which was a clear river, not polluted.
It had beaches.
And when I was young, I was sent to camp, boys' camp, on the Vistula river also.
No, but I know the village.
I mean, the town, that was.
It was, the town was Swider.
S as Sam, but the S with an apostrophe, which is become soft S, shh.
W-I, D as David, E-R, Swider.
Oh, I would say it was in '36, or '35, '36, when I went to boys' camps.
Later we were together in it, and went also further away in Poland.
Yes.
Yes.
And I recall I had a wonderful mother, I mean, very unique.
Not only was she loving, but she gave me full independence.
There was nothing that I could do wrong, unless I harmed somebody or did something bad to somebody.
No matter how I came home, after a fight bleeding, never questions me.
But I recall in a camp when the parents came visiting, mothers, and the kids were very nervous and wouldn't, during dinner, wouldn't eat anything, you know.
And their mothers kept nagging at them, why don't you eat.
And my mother never during my history ever asked me to eat.
If you don't want to eat, you don't have to.
You just don't get anything in between till the next meal.
We had no snacks in between.
So don't eat, you don't have to.
Never questioning, never nag me on it.
She was, she gave me full independence.
And it helped me later in life, you know, that I could rely on myself.
Perhaps, in my closest family, or brother and sister, you're talking about, or further, cousins?
The outside, extended family-- grandparents, aunts, uncles, cousins.
Yes, my mother, my mother's side grandparents, her father has passed away before my memory.
Her mother, my grandmother, as I was a little boy, I recall that on a vacation, I used to sleep with her.
Or she used to take me to her bed.
She died in bed with me.
Oh, must have been early '30s, '32, '30--
--'33, in a vacation.
I don't know what village or what town it was, but I remember she passed away.
I was sleeping with her and she just passed away at night.
I don't recall it.
I don't recall it.
But, of course, she lived with us, the grandmother, mother's side.
The closest we were, of course, was to the mother's side of the family, because she visited there more often than the father's side of the family.
Although, my grandparents from the father's side lived, lived in the same apartment building, two floors beneath us, or below us, [INAUDIBLE] was below us on the first floor.
We were in a third floor.
So we very often, I went back and forth.
But the closest relations we had with the family, cousins, and uncle, and aunt, who are from my mother's side.
I don't recall her first name, but the last name was Benderman.
Her maiden name was Gold, as my mother.
He was a sister of my mother, Benderman.
In fact, their son, I don't know if I should get you, the son is now in Israel.
He left Poland in 1938.
He couldn't get to Polish university.
There was [INAUDIBLE].
They would not accept Jews into technical school.
He was, he wanted to study aerodynamics or whatever.
He went, he had some cousin Australia.
He went to Australia and he survived.
He fought with Australian army.
Then he joined Israeli army or prior to Israel, Palestine.
And he lives now retired in a kibbutz.
His name was Benderman, Mark Benderman.
Now he changed his name to Mark Bendor.
Bendor is B as in Boy, E-N, D as David, O-R, Bendor.
He was a major in Israeli army.
He fought through all the wars.
Yes.
My grandmother as I recalled, her first name was Pesah.
Yes, ?] grandfather was Godel, G-O-D-E-L.
P as in Peter, E-S-A-H.
We had good relation.
Of course, I had constant fight with kids, unfortunately.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There was a great amount of anti-Semitism in Poland.
I myself was very much Polish.
By the way, our family was in Poland since 14th century.
We had dates going back, and back, and back.
And of course, in any other country, anybody living that long would be considered a citizen, a part, you know, [? in ?] France, or Britain, or United States.
Not so in Poland.
You are a Jew.
You are not a Pole.
Although I was a Pole.
I spoke only Polish language.
I love the literature.
And I'm sort of a patriot.
Not withstanding that, the kids were thought, both in school, in parochial school, to which I also went, by the way.
I went to a Catholic parochial school.
Taught all the-- by the-- by the church, the Christ killers, the [INAUDIBLE], the killers, the father killers.
And anti-Semitism was almost inbred, almost, by osmosis passed on.
So we had fight with the kids, not with the neighbors.
